name: Generated Essays Were Human-Validated for Score Comparability and Realism
statement: Wolfe and Barber generated simulated essays from same-score real essays and used expert raters to score them and judge realism, positioning the work as calibration for future automated scoring engine data augmentation.
quantitative_note: Predict Next simulations had score agreement comparable to real essays; expert raters identified only 25% of Predict Next simulations and 18% of 25 Examples simulations as simulated.
evaluation_method: Human score agreement, QWK, average score comparisons, and real/simulated classification accuracy.
sparsity_or_imbalance_motivation: Limited training data and sparse observations at some score points can impede automated scoring engine development.
synthetic_data_research_role: Synthetic responses were used to validate generation methods, not directly to validate a deployed scorer.
reported_metrics: Agreement proportions, QWK, average score comparisons, real/simulated classification accuracy.
limitations: The study did not train a downstream automated scoring engine with the generated essays.
It is bounded to one writing prompt, grade level, rubric, and state context.

source_locator.section: Abstract; Process; Scoring and Realism Rating; Analyses; Discussion and Implications
source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase_note: The finding paraphrases the full-text sections describing simulated essay generation, expert scoring, realism ratings, agreement analyses, and data-augmentation positioning.
